intro:
Mahlah (meaning disease or mildness) was the eldest of the five daughters of Zelophehad of the tribe of Manasseh, who died in the wilderness leaving no sons (Numbers 27:1). Mahlah and her sisters — Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — petitioned Moses before the congregation, asking for their father's inheritance among his brothers. God directed Moses to give them their father's portion, establishing a legal precedent in Israel's inheritance law: daughters inherit when there are no sons, with the restriction that they must marry within their own tribe to preserve the territorial integrity of tribal allotments (Numbers 36).

The case of Zelophehad's daughters was legally significant enough to be mentioned three times in Numbers (27:1; 36:1–12) and once in Joshua (17:3–6), where they successfully claim their inheritance in Canaan. Their names recur in each mention, unusual for women in the biblical legal texts and reflecting the importance of their precedent-setting case. Mahlah's name heads every listing of the five sisters.
